title: LICENSE file is valid MIT with Sixty North AS attribution preserved
description: The LICENSE file contains standard MIT text with dual copyright: 'Copyright (c) 2019 Sixty North AS' and 'Copyright (c) 2019-2026 Isaac Douglas'. It also includes a provenance note linking to the upstream fork. This satisfies D-009 requirements.
verification_note: Confirmed. LICENSE file has dual copyright (Sixty North AS + Isaac Douglas), provenance link to sixty-north/python-camtasia, and standard MIT text. Satisfies D-009.

title: All core and optional dependencies are MIT-compatible
description: Core deps: jsonpatch (BSD), typing_extensions (PSF-2.0). CLI extras: exit-codes (MIT), docopt-subcommands (MIT). Media extras: pymediainfo (MIT). Docs extras: sphinx (BSD-2-Clause), furo (MIT), myst-parser (MIT), sphinx-autodoc-typehints (MIT). Dev/test extras: ruff (MIT), pytest (MIT), hypothesis (MPL-2.0, weak copyleft — test-only, not distributed), mypy (MIT), jsonschema (MIT). No GPL-only or AGPL dependencies found.
verification_note: Confirmed. All listed dependencies have MIT-compatible licenses. Core deps are jsonpatch (BSD) and typing_extensions (PSF). No GPL-only or AGPL dependencies in the dependency tree. Positive finding.

title: docutils transitive dependency lists GPL among license options
description: docutils (transitive via sphinx) reports license as 'BSD License; GNU General Public License (GPL); Public Domain'. This is a tri-licensed package where the user may choose BSD or Public Domain, both MIT-compatible. However, the license field is ambiguous in pip-licenses output. This is a docs-only transitive dependency and not distributed with pycamtasia. Risk is negligible but worth documenting.
verification_note: Confirmed. docutils is tri-licensed (BSD/GPL/Public Domain) — user can choose BSD which is MIT-compatible. This is a docs-only transitive dependency not distributed with the package. Risk is negligible. Valid informational finding.

title: No copyright/license headers in any source files (92 .py files)
description: Zero of 92 Python source files under src/camtasia/ contain copyright headers, license identifiers, or SPDX tags. While the top-level LICENSE file covers the project, per-file headers are a best practice for MIT-licensed projects — especially forks — to ensure attribution survives when individual files are copied or extracted. The Sixty North AS attribution from D-009 is only in the LICENSE file, not in any source file.
verification_note: Confirmed. project.py (and by extension all source files) starts with a module docstring, not a copyright header. No SPDX tags or license identifiers in any source file. The D-009 Sixty North AS attribution exists only in the top-level LICENSE file.

title: TechSmith fixture files lack redistribution license documentation
description: Three test fixtures (techsmith_sample.tscproj, techsmith_library_asset.tscproj, techsmith_complex_asset.tscproj) appear to be derived from TechSmith Camtasia project output. There is no documentation of the right to redistribute these files. TechSmith's EULA typically restricts redistribution of application output formats. If these were generated by the project author using a licensed copy of Camtasia, that should be documented. If they contain TechSmith proprietary content, a license grant or fair-use justification is needed.
verification_note: Confirmed. Three techsmith_*.tscproj files exist in tests/fixtures/ (326K, 156K, 679K). No accompanying LICENSE, README, or documentation explaining their provenance or redistribution rights. These are included in the sdist (REV-packaging-004) and thus distributed to PyPI users.

title: Bundled Camtasia project template in resources/ lacks license documentation
description: src/camtasia/resources/new.cmproj/ contains a template Camtasia project (project.tscproj, docPrefs, bookmarks.plist) that is distributed with the package (via package-data in pyproject.toml). The template references 'Camtasia' and 'TechSmith' in its authoringClientName field. There is no documentation of whether this template was created from scratch, generated by Camtasia, or derived from TechSmith materials. If generated by Camtasia, TechSmith's EULA may restrict redistribution.
verification_note: Confirmed. resources/new.cmproj/ contains project.tscproj, docPrefs, bookmarks.plist, and a media/ directory. This template is distributed with the wheel and sdist. No license documentation or provenance note accompanies it.

title: JSON schema in resources/ has no license or attribution
description: src/camtasia/resources/camtasia-project-schema.json is a 42KB schema file describing TechSmith's .tscproj format. The $id field references 'pycamtasia.readthedocs.io', suggesting it was authored by the project. However, the schema describes a proprietary file format and has no license header or attribution. If the schema was reverse-engineered, that's likely fine under interoperability exceptions, but should be documented.
verification_note: Confirmed. Schema $id is 'https://pycamtasia.readthedocs.io/camtasia-project-schema.json', indicating project authorship. No license header in the file. The schema describes a reverse-engineered format — interoperability exceptions likely apply but documentation would strengthen the position.

title: pyproject.toml license metadata uses new PEP 639 format correctly
description: The project uses license = 'MIT' and license-files = ['LICENSE'], which is the PEP 639 format supported by setuptools>=77. This is correct and consistent with the LICENSE file content. The authors field lists only Isaac Douglas — Sixty North AS is not listed as an author (correctly, since they are a copyright holder via the LICENSE, not a current author).
verification_note: Confirmed. pyproject.toml has `license = "MIT"` and `license-files = ["LICENSE"]`. PEP 639 format is correct. Positive finding — no action needed.
